metadata.summary: Grafana open-source observability platform for visualizing metrics, logs, and traces across Prometheus, Loki, Elasticsearch, and other data sources with alerting.

content.title: Grafana Basics
content.summary: Grafana is an open-source observability platform for visualizing metrics, logs, and traces. It supports multiple data sources (Prometheus, Loki, Elasticsearch, InfluxDB), provides rich visualization options, and enables alerting based on dashboard panels. Treat dashboards as code from day one: generate JSON, review via API or Grafonnet, never hand-click in the UI.

why.p: Grafana turns silent time-series data into actionable dashboards for on-call engineers. It unifies metrics (Prometheus/Mimir), logs (Loki), and traces (Tempo) in a single pane, reducing context switches during incidents. Dashboard provisioning via JSON in Git means dashboards survive reinstalls, version-control changes, and multi-environment promotions. The RED/USE/Four Golden Signals frameworks give per-service observability a consistent structure that new team members can learn once and apply everywhere.

list.item: Visualizing Prometheus metrics
Creating operational dashboards
Building SLO/SLI dashboards
Log analysis with Loki
Unified observability across multiple data sources
Real-time monitoring and incident response
Surfacing Prometheus / Loki / Tempo data the team already collects but never looks at — turning silent metrics into a dashboard the on-call actually opens
Building per-service RED/USE/Golden-Signals dashboards as part of a service onboarding template, so every new service ships with the same five panels
SLO/error-budget visualization once recording rules exist (job:slo_burn:ratio_rate1h)
Incident-response timelines: combining annotations (deploys, releases) with metrics on a single graph
Loki log exploration with LogQL next to the metric panel that triggered an alert (split-pane view)
As the metrics store itself — Grafana is a rendering layer; a missing datasource or a slow Prometheus is the real failure
For long-term query performance audits — use pprof/Mimir directly, not the dashboard
When you need transactional accuracy (counts must match invoicing) — time-series + downsampling produces approximations
As a CMDB or service catalog — use Backstage / Cortex / Port; Grafana drops "soft" labels
Single-pane-of-glass for security events (SIEM) — use Grafana for ops; route auth/audit logs to a SIEM
Dashboard — Collection of panels organized in rows
Panel — Individual visualization (graph, table, stat, gauge)
Data Source — Backend providing data (Prometheus, Loki, InfluxDB)
Variable — Dynamic values for filtering dashboards
Alert — Condition-based notifications from panels
Annotation — Event markers on graphs
Folder — Organizational container for dashboards
Playlist — Auto-rotating dashboard slideshow
Time series — Metrics over time — Best for: Trend analysis, rate monitoring
Stat — Single value display — Best for: KPIs, current values
Gauge — Value within range — Best for: SLO compliance, utilization
Bar gauge — Comparative values — Best for: Side-by-side comparisons
Table — Tabular data — Best for: Pod status, detailed metrics
Heatmap — Distribution over time — Best for: Request latency distribution
Logs — Log aggregation display — Best for: Loki integration
Pie chart — Proportional data — Best for: Resource distribution
Geomap — Location-based data — Best for: Geographic metrics

section.name: observability-frameworks
section.p: RED Method (Request-driven): Rate — Requests per second — Time series panel; Errors — Error rate percentage — Stat + threshold; Duration — Request latency (p50, p95, p99) — Time series, heatmap.
USE Method (Resource-driven): Utilization — % time resource busy — Gauge; Saturation — Queue depth, wait time — Time series; Errors — Error counts — Stat.
Four Golden Signals: Latency — Time to serve request; Traffic — Demand on system; Errors — Rate of failed requests; Saturation — How "full" the service is.

example.title: Prometheus data source provisioning
example.code: apiVersion: 1 datasources: - name: Prometheus type: prometheus access: proxy url: http://prometheus:9090 isDefault: true jsonData: timeInterval: "15s" httpMethod: POST

example.title: Loki data source provisioning
example.code: apiVersion: 1 datasources: - name: Loki type: loki access: proxy url: http://loki:3100 jsonData: maxLines: 1000

rule.statement: Every dashboard goes through provisioning. UI changes are a smell: export, diff, PR.
rule.rationale: Dashboards built ad-hoc in the UI drift away from JSON in Git and are lost on next provisioning sync.

rule.statement: Variables use label_values() filtered by parent variables, never hand-typed lists.
rule.rationale: Hand-typed lists go stale; label_values() dynamically reflects what's actually scraped.

rule.statement: Threshold colors: green / amber / red mapped to SLO bands, not arbitrary numbers; store the SLO target in a constant variable ($slo_target).
rule.rationale: Consistent threshold semantics across dashboards reduce cognitive overhead when triage-ing multiple services simultaneously.

rule.statement: Always link related dashboards (Dashboard links): service → host → cluster → company-wide overview. Drill-down in three clicks.
rule.rationale: Without navigation links, engineers miss context that lives one level higher or lower in the hierarchy.

rule.statement: Use recording rules (job:rate5m, job:errors:ratio_rate5m) — never compute heavy expressions inside a dashboard query.
rule.rationale: Complex PromQL evaluated at dashboard refresh time multiplies cost by concurrent user count; recording rules pre-compute once.

rule.statement: Set the data-source as a variable ($datasource), so the same JSON works in dev/staging/prod.
rule.rationale: Hardcoded datasource UIDs break the same dashboard in other environments.

rule.statement: Use variables to enable filtering by namespace, pod, environment.
rule.rationale: Static dashboards don't scale as services multiply; variables allow one dashboard to serve all instances.

rule.statement: Set appropriate time ranges matched to dashboard purpose (real-time vs historical).
rule.rationale: Mismatch between refresh rate and data update frequency creates misleading "stale" or thrashing panels.

rule.statement: Use consistent colors — red for errors, green for success across dashboards.
rule.rationale: Inconsistent color semantics force engineers to re-read legends under incident pressure, slowing triage.

rule.statement: Add descriptions — panel descriptions explain what metrics mean.
rule.rationale: Context lost when original author moves on; descriptions are the only inline documentation visible during an incident.

rule.statement: Include annotations — mark deployments, incidents on graphs.
rule.rationale: Annotations provide instant correlation between operational events and metric changes without manual cross-referencing.

rule.statement: dashboard-linter in CI is non-negotiable — agents pass it once and forget it; pin the linter version so rule changes don't surprise.
rule.rationale: Linting catches panel configuration anti-patterns, missing units, and PromQL foot-guns before they reach production.

list.item: Too many panels — Information overload — Solution: Focus on key metrics per dashboard
Missing variables — Static dashboards don't scale — Solution: Add namespace/pod filters
No thresholds — Requires mental calculation — Solution: Set visual thresholds
Inconsistent naming — Hard to find dashboards — Solution: Use naming conventions
Manual creation — Changes lost on reinstall — Solution: Use provisioning
Complex panel queries — Slow dashboards — Solution: Use recording rules
No documentation — Context lost — Solution: Add Text panels, descriptions
Alert fatigue — Ignored notifications — Solution: Symptom-based alerting
Dashboards built ad-hoc in the UI drift away from JSON in Git — agents that "just edit the dashboard" lose changes on next provisioning sync.
Variable misuse: $namespace defined as multi-value but the panel query uses =~"$namespace" without regex_replace_chars. A namespace named prod-eu becomes a regex that matches anywhere.
Heatmap panels need histogram metrics with le buckets — agents replace histogram_quantile with quantile (a different function) and the latency p95 silently misreports.
Stat panels with "Last (not null)" reducer cache stale values during scrape gaps; the dashboard looks healthy while the service is dead.
Time-zone confusion: dashboard default vs user override vs alert evaluation TZ — three different "now"s.
Alert rules defined inside dashboard panels (legacy "Dashboard alerts") are not the same surface as Grafana Alerting / Prometheus rules; they're fragile and easy to lose.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: Treat dashboards as code from day one. One agent generates a dashboard JSON from a service spec (RED + golden signals + variables + thresholds), a reviewer agent (Opus) checks PromQL against actual metric names exposed by the service (/metrics curl) and rejects panels that reference non-existent metrics. A third agent diffs the new JSON against the existing provisioned file and produces a Grafana folder PR. Never let an agent click in the UI; always go through grafana-cli, the HTTP API, or Grafonnet.

Recommended subagents: faion-sdd-executor-agent for driving spec → JSON → review → provision PR; custom dashboard-promql-validator (Opus, read-only) given dashboard JSON + a metric name list, linting every panel query, flagging missing labels, regex foot-guns, and quantile-vs-histogram_quantile confusion; password-scrubber-agent for datasource provisioning files that often inline basic-auth tokens.

list.item: Set up GitHub Actions workflow from template — haiku: Pattern application, simple configuration
Design CI/CD pipeline architecture — opus: Complex system design with many variables
Write terraform code for infrastructure — sonnet: Implementation with moderate complexity
Debug failing pipeline step — sonnet: Debugging and problem-solving
Implement AIOps anomaly detection — opus: Novel ML approach, complex decision
Configure webhook and secret management — haiku: Mechanical setup using checklists
Agents fabricate metric names that "sound right" (http_request_count vs the actual http_requests_total) — always validate against /metrics before merging.
LLMs reach for irate(...[1m]) because it appears in tutorials, then dashboards flicker on slow scrape intervals. Force rate(...[$__rate_interval]).
Multi-value variables expand to a regex; agents wire = instead of =~. The dashboard works for one selection and silently breaks for two.
LLMs recreate Prometheus metrics inside the dashboard ("calculate p95 from raw events") instead of using the existing _bucket series — produces wrong numbers and slow queries.
Dashboards generated from natural language tend to overfit the demo: hardcoded namespace="default", instance="localhost:9090". Force every panel to reference a variable.
Human-in-loop checkpoint: any new dashboard goes to a reviewer who sanity-checks SLO mapping and verifies the dashboard renders with empty/no-data series (not just the happy path).

checklist.check: Set refresh interval based on data update frequency
Place most important metrics at top
Use recording rules for complex queries
Use instant queries for tables where appropriate
Test query performance with large time ranges
Define green/yellow/red thresholds aligned with SLO targets
Use consistent threshold colors across dashboards
Test threshold visibility at different values
Add Text panel for dashboard instructions
Set appropriate severity based on user impact
Group related alerts to reduce noise
Set max lines limit for Loki
Configure derived fields in Loki if needed

example.title: Request Rate stat panel
Error Rate Percentage stat panel
Request Rate by Status Code time series
Latency Percentiles p50/p95/p99
SLO Availability Gauge (99.9%)
CPU Utilization Gauge
Pod Status Table
Top 10 Endpoints by P99 Latency Table
Application Logs panel (Loki)
Error Logs only panel (Loki)
Request Latency Distribution Heatmap
Deployment and Alert annotations
Complete Application Overview Dashboard (RED method)
Variable templates: namespace, service (cascading), environment
example.code: { "namespace_variable": { "name": "namespace", "label": "Namespace", "type": "query", "datasource": {"type": "prometheus", "uid": "${datasource}"}, "query": "label_values(kube_namespace_labels, namespace)", "refresh": 1, "sort": 1, "includeAll": false, "multi": false, "regex": "", "hide": 0 }, "service_variable": { "name": "service", "label": "Service", "type": "query", "datasource": {"type": "prometheus", "uid": "${datasource}"}, "query": "label_values(kube_service_info{namespace=\"$namespace\"}, service)", "refresh": 2, "sort": 1, "includeAll": true, "multi": true, "allValue": ".*" }, "environment_variable": { "name": "environment", "label": "Environment", "type": "custom", "options": [ {"text": "Production", "value": "production", "selected": true}, {"text": "Staging", "value": "staging", "selected": false}, {"text": "Development", "value": "development", "selected": false} ], "includeAll": false, "multi": false } }

example.title: High Error Rate Alert Rule
High P99 Latency Alert Rule
Pod Not Ready Alert Rule
Prometheus with Prometheus type and alerting
example.code: apiVersion: 1 datasources: - name: Prometheus type: prometheus access: proxy url: http://prometheus-server:9090 isDefault: true editable: false jsonData: timeInterval: "15s" httpMethod: POST manageAlerts: true prometheusType: Prometheus prometheusVersion: "2.45.0"

example.title: Loki with derived fields for trace correlation
example.code: apiVersion: 1 datasources: - name: Loki type: loki access: proxy url: http://loki:3100 editable: false jsonData: maxLines: 1000 derivedFields: - name: TraceID matcherRegex: "traceID=(\\w+)" url: "$${__value.raw}" datasourceUid: tempo

example.title: Tempo with trace-to-logs correlation
example.code: apiVersion: 1 datasources: - name: Tempo type: tempo access: proxy url: http://tempo:3200 editable: false jsonData: httpMethod: GET tracesToLogs: datasourceUid: loki tags: ['job', 'instance', 'pod', 'namespace'] mappedTags: [{ key: 'service.name', value: 'service' }] mapTagNamesEnabled: true spanStartTimeShift: '-1h' spanEndTimeShift: '1h' filterByTraceID: true filterBySpanID: false

example.title: Dashboard provider configuration
example.code: apiVersion: 1 providers: - name: 'default' orgId: 1 folder: 'Infrastructure' folderUid: 'infrastructure' type: file disableDeletion: false updateIntervalSeconds: 30 allowUiUpdates: true options: path: /var/lib/grafana/dashboards foldersFromFilesStructure: true

example.title: Slack and PagerDuty contact points
example.code: apiVersion: 1 contactPoints: - orgId: 1 name: slack-alerts receivers: - uid: slack-critical type: slack settings: url: ${SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL} recipient: "#alerts-critical" username: Grafana icon_emoji: ":alert:" mentionChannel: "here" disableResolveMessage: false - orgId: 1 name: pagerduty-critical receivers: - uid: pagerduty-1 type: pagerduty settings: integrationKey: ${PAGERDUTY_INTEGRATION_KEY} severity: critical class: "infrastructure" component: "{{ .Labels.service }}"

section.name: llm-prompts-dashboard
section.prompt:
Create a Grafana dashboard JSON for monitoring a {service_type} application with:
Application: {app_name} Namespace variable: yes Data source: Prometheus
Include panels for: 1. Overview row with stat panels: - Request rate (req/s) - Error rate (%) - P99 latency (seconds) - Active pods count
2. Traffic row with time series: - Request rate by status code (2xx green, 4xx yellow, 5xx red) - Request rate by endpoint
3. Latency row: - Latency percentiles (p50, p95, p99) - Latency heatmap
4. Resources row: - CPU utilization by pod - Memory utilization by pod
5. Logs row (Loki): - Application logs filtered by namespace
Use these metrics: - HTTP: http_requests_total, http_request_duration_seconds_bucket - K8s: kube_pod_info, kube_pod_status_phase, container_cpu_usage_seconds_total, container_memory_usage_bytes
Add deployment annotations. Set 30s refresh rate. Use $__rate_interval for rate() functions.

Create a Grafana SLO dashboard JSON for:
Service: {service_name} SLO targets: - Availability: {availability_target}% (e.g., 99.9%) - Latency P99: {latency_target}ms - Error budget period: {period} (e.g., 30d)
Include: 1. Gauge panels showing current SLO compliance 2. Error budget remaining (percentage and time) 3. Burn rate indicators (1h, 6h, 24h windows) 4. Time series showing SLI trends over the budget period 5. Alert status panel
Use Prometheus metrics: - http_requests_total for availability - http_request_duration_seconds_bucket for latency
Color thresholds: - Green: meeting SLO - Yellow: within 10% of SLO breach - Red: SLO breached

section.name: llm-prompts-panels
llm-prompts-alerts
llm-prompts-variables-and-transforms
section.prompt:
Create Grafana variable definitions for cascading filters:
1. datasource variable (prometheus type) 2. namespace variable (query from kube_namespace_labels) 3. service variable (filtered by $namespace) 4. pod variable (filtered by $namespace and $service, multi-select, include all)
All variables should: - Refresh on dashboard load or time range change as appropriate - Be sorted alphabetically - Have clear labels

Analyze this Grafana dashboard and suggest optimizations:
{dashboard_json}
Check for: 1. Queries that could use recording rules 2. Panels with unnecessary high cardinality 3. Excessive refresh rates 4. Missing instant query flags on tables 5. Redundant panels that could be combined 6. Variables that refresh too frequently
Provide: - List of issues found - Suggested recording rules (PromQL) - Optimized dashboard JSON

section.name: llm-prompts-datasources
section.prompt:
Create Grafana data source provisioning YAML for Prometheus with:
URL: {prometheus_url} Features: - Exemplars enabled, linked to Tempo - Custom scrape interval: {interval} - HTTP POST method - Alerting enabled
Include correlations to Loki for logs.

Create Grafana data source provisioning YAML for Loki with:
URL: {loki_url} Features: - Derived field to extract trace IDs - Link to Tempo for distributed tracing - Max lines: 1000
Pattern: traceID=(\w+) or trace_id=(\w+)

section.name: llm-prompts-troubleshooting
section.prompt:
Migrate this Grafana 9 dashboard to Grafana 11:
{old_dashboard_json}
Update: - Schema version to 39 - Angular panels to React equivalents - Deprecated panel options - Alert rule format to unified alerting - Variable syntax if needed
Output the updated JSON with migration notes.

section.name: llm-prompt-patterns
section.prompt: Generate Grafana dashboard JSON for service <X>. Inputs: service name, metric prefix, owner team, SLO target, scrape interval. Output: (1) variables ($env, $namespace, $instance), (2) RED row (rate, errors%, latency p50/p95/p99), (3) USE row (CPU/mem/saturation), (4) deploy/incident annotations from `events` datasource, (5) thresholds wired to SLO. Forbid: dashboard-internal alerts, hardcoded namespaces, multi-value vars without `regex_replace_chars`, `quantile()` on `_bucket` metrics.
Validate dashboard JSON against `curl http://svc/metrics`. For every panel, list (panel_id, query, missing_metrics[], missing_labels[]). Reject if any list non-empty.

example.title: Grafonnet RED dashboard skeleton (Jsonnet)

list.item: grafana-cli — Plugin install, admin reset, db migration — https://grafana.com/docs/grafana/latest/cli/
grizzly (grr) — Apply Grafana resources from YAML/Jsonnet (kubectl-like) — https://grafana.github.io/grizzly/
grafonnet — Jsonnet library for dashboard generation — https://grafana.github.io/grafonnet/
jsonnet / jb (jsonnet-bundler) — Build Grafonnet output — https://github.com/jsonnet-bundler/jsonnet-bundler
Grafana HTTP API — CRUD dashboards/folders/datasources via curl — https://grafana.com/docs/grafana/latest/developers/http_api/
promtool check rules — Validate PromQL in recording/alert rules used by panels — https://prometheus.io/docs/prometheus/latest/command-line/promtool/
dashboard-linter (Grafana Labs) — Lints JSON dashboards for best practices — https://github.com/grafana/dashboard-linter
Grafana OSS — OSS, agent-friendly — Provisioning + HTTP API + service accounts
Grafana Cloud — SaaS, agent-friendly — Free tier sufficient for solo projects; API tokens scoped per stack
Grafana Enterprise — SaaS/self-hosted, agent-friendly — Adds RBAC, reporting, data-source permissions
Mimir / Cortex / Thanos — OSS, agent-friendly — Long-term Prometheus storage; same PromQL surface
Loki — OSS / SaaS, agent-friendly — LogQL queries inside Grafana panels
Tempo — OSS / SaaS, agent-friendly — Trace links from logs/metrics via derivedFields
Pyroscope — OSS / SaaS, partial agent-friendly — Continuous profiling source; agent must understand flame-graph panel
